original_abstract: The efficient cleavage of C−C bonds and the complete oxidation of alcohols to CO₂ are critical hurdles in the development of high-performance fuel cells for renewable energy. Here, we report the synthesis of ultrathin (2.3 nm) core/shell Au/Pt nanowires (NWs) featuring stepped Pt clusters deposited along a stepped gold core. These specialized architectures serve as highly efficient catalysts for total alcohol oxidation. We demonstrate that catalytic performance is sensitive to the Au/Pt atomic ratio; specifically, Au₁.₀/Pt₀.₂ NWs—which possess the highest density of stepped Au/Pt sites (~75%)—exhibit an unprecedented mass activity of 196.9 A/mgPt (32.5 A/mgPt+Au) for ethanol electro-oxidation. This catalytic system also proves exceptionally effective for the oxidation of other primary alcohols, including methanol, n-propanol, and ethylene glycol. Using in situ X-ray absorption and infrared spectroscopies, we identified key reaction intermediates and characterized the catalyst structure, providing direct evidence that the synergy between low-coordinated Pt sites and the stepped Au surface is fundamental to the reaction mechanism. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations further corroborate that these undercoordinated Pt-Au surfaces significantly lower the energy barrier for C−C bond cleavage. This work highlights the design of stepped core/shell interfaces as a powerful strategy for enhancing electrochemical oxidation and provides a roadmap for the future development of highly efficient direct alcohol fuel cells.
humanized_abstract: The selective cleavage of C−C bonds and the complete oxidation of alcohols to CO₂ are two of the biggest challenges for next generation low temperature fuel cells, particularly direct liquid feed fuel cells, that can efficiently utilize the renewable energy resource of alcohol biofuel. Here we design ultrathin core/shell Au/Pt nanowires (NWs) with stepped clusters of Pt on a stepped gold core, which perform as very efficient catalysts for the total oxidation of alcohols. The catalytic performance is clearly dependent on the Au/Pt composition, and our optimum catalyst, Au₁.₀/Pt₀.₂ NWs with the highest density of active sites, i.e. ~75% stepped Au/Pt, gives a mass activity of 196.9 A/mgPt (32.5 A/mgPt+Au) for ethanol oxidation. It also catalyses the oxidation of all of the primary alcohols we tested including methanol, n-propanol and ethylene glycol. We used in situ XAS and IR to show that the catalytic sites involve a synergy between low-coordinated Pt sites and a stepped Au surface that can accommodate the intermediates involved in alcohol electro-oxidation reactions. DFT studies confirmed the activity enhancement of undercoordinated Pt-Au sites due to their lower activation energy barriers for the C−C bond cleavage. This study design core/shell interfaces with stepped structures between Au and Pt as high performance catalysts for the electro-oxidation of alcohols and thus points the way towards design of next generation of direct alcohol fuel cells.
